body:
![security-high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/security-high-priority.svg) ![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

The `buildCardHtml` function is vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). User-provided strings such as `card.tag`, `card.title`, `card.subtitle`, and button labels are injected directly into the HTML template without any escaping. Since this is an editor where users can input arbitrary text, an attacker could inject malicious scripts. You should implement a helper function to escape HTML special characters (e.g., `<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, `'`) before inserting them into the template literal.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The DOM-based fallback in `parseSlides` is incomplete. It only attempts to recover the `title` and `copyable` properties, meaning that if the `data-card-slides` attribute is missing or corrupted, all other data (tags, subtitles, info lines, and buttons) will be lost when the editor is opened. The parser should be updated to extract all fields defined in the `CardSlide` interface from the existing DOM structure to ensure data persistence.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The `SLIDE_SCRIPT` appends a new `<style>` element to the component root every time it initializes. While the editor cleans up styles in `applyToBlock`, the `ViewClient` (used for previewing) only replaces scripts and does not remove these dynamically added styles. This results in multiple redundant `<style>` tags accumulating in the DOM during a long editing session. Consider making the script idempotent by checking for an existing style tag (e.g., using a specific data attribute) before appending a new one.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

There is significant code duplication between this file and `scripts/migrate-info-card-slide-to-html.ts`. The `CardSlide` interface, `buildCardHtml`, `sanitizeHref`, and `SLIDE_SCRIPT` are identical in both places. This duplication increases the risk of bugs if the component structure is updated in one file but not the other. These shared elements should be moved to a common utility or data file to adhere to the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle.
